summary: Gruber methodically exposes the inconsistency in Apple's App Store age ratings for Wikipedia-based apps. Using a structured, factual format, he shows that Wikipanion is rated 17+ while Kiwi and Wikipedia Mobile are rated 4+, despite all three providing access to identical content. He ties this to Phil Schiller's defense of the 17+ rating given to the dictionary app Ninjawords for containing vulgar urban slang terms. By demonstrating that the same offensive terms are accessible in all three Wikipedia apps regardless of rating, he undermines Apple's stated rationale. He concedes the terms aren't appropriate for children, but makes clear that the real issue is arbitrary, inconsistent enforcement.
key_points: Three Wikipedia client apps provide access to identical content but have wildly different age ratings (4+ vs 17+)
Apple's Phil Schiller defended the Ninjawords 17+ rating by citing access to vulgar urban slang not found in traditional dictionaries, but Wikipedia apps expose the same terms
The structured, just-the-facts format lets the inconsistency speak for itself without requiring editorial commentary
Gruber acknowledges the content genuinely isn't appropriate for children, separating the appropriateness question from the consistency question
money_quotes: Apparent difference in available content in all three apps: None.
Consistency in the age ratings for Wikipedia-based apps in App Store: None.
Sympathy, on the part of yours truly, that these terms are not in fact appropriate for young children: Much.
The issue that the App Store reviewers did find with the Ninjawords application is that it provided access to other more vulgar terms than those found in traditional and common dictionaries.
Number defined in Mac OS X's New Oxford American Dictionary: Zero.
key_insight: Apple's App Store age rating system is applied so inconsistently that apps providing identical access to objectionable content receive wildly different ratings, undermining the company's own stated rationale for its review decisions.
